subject: [PATCH] ACPI: NUMA: Only parse CFMWS at boot when CXL_ACPI is on

reviews.author: Huang, Kai
reviews.summary:
Reviewer asked if other modules besides CXL_ACPI use the CFMWS information for CXL memory regions, probing the implications of only parsing CFMWS at boot when CXL_ACPI is enabled

Reviewer noted that the patch only avoids detecting CFMWS, which doesn't provide any NUMA information, and therefore kernel assigns a 'faked' NUMA node for each of them, not actually losing any NUMA info.
sentiment_signals: no clear signal from reviewer's comment
reviews.raw_body:
Besides CXL_ACPI, is there any other module(s) that uses this information
for these CXL memory regions?

---

The normal NUMA info described in SRAT is still there.  It only avoids
detecting CFMWS, which doesn't provide any NUMA info actually -- that's why
kernel assigns a 'faked' NUMA node for each of them.

So we are not losing anything AFAICT.

reviews.author: Huang, Kai
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Huang, Kai questioned the 'strange' behavior of the patch and provided ACPI table dumps to demonstrate that CEDT tables are present on the platform, suggesting that the issue may not be as described in the patch.

Reviewer noted that the patch's logic to only parse CFMWS at boot when CXL_ACPI is on causes a problem where the NUMA structure is lost even though it's accurately described by BIOS, and suggested re-evaluating whether this issue should be handled in the acpi code or if lazy-creating these things would be a better approach.

Reviewer questioned the rationality of permanently losing NUMA structure information when CXL_ACPI is built, suggesting it might be better to lazy-create these entries instead

Reviewer noted that the patch's decision to only parse CFMWS at boot when CXL_ACPI is on incurs a cost if CXL_ACPI is not compiled out by default, and requested justification for this design choice.
reviews.raw_body:
How to check whether it is "strange"?

I dumped the ACPI table on that platform, and what I can see is there's CEDT
table which contains couple of "[CXL Host Bridge Structure]" and "[CXL Fixed
Memory Window Structure]".

I pasted one for each of them here FYI:

[024h 0036 001h]               Subtable Type : 00 [CXL Host Bridge
Structure]
[025h 0037 001h]                    Reserved : 00
[026h 0038 002h]                      Length : 0020
[028h 0040 004h]      Associated host bridge : 00000003
[02Ch 0044 004h]       Specification version : 00000001
[030h 0048 004h]                    Reserved : 00000000
[034h 0052 008h]               Register base : 00000000997F0000
[03Ch 0060 008h]             Register length : 0000000000010000

[124h 0292 001h]               Subtable Type : 01 [CXL Fixed Memory Window
Structure]
[125h 0293 001h]                    Reserved : 00
[126h 0294 002h]                      Length : 0034
[128h 0296 004h]                    Reserved : 00000000
[12Ch 0300 008h]         Window base address : 0000002080000000
[134h 0308 008h]                 Window size : 0000017B00000000
[13Ch 0316 001h]    Interleave Members (2^n) : 02
[13Dh 0317 001h]       Interleave Arithmetic : 01
[13Eh 0318 002h]                    Reserved : 0000
[140h 0320 004h]                 Granularity : 00000000
[144h 0324 002h]                Restrictions : 0006
[146h 0326 002h]                       QtgId : 0000
[148h 0328 004h]                First Target : 00000003
[14Ch 0332 004h]                 Next Target : 00000023
[150h 0336 004h]                 Next Target : 00000043
[154h 0340 004h]                 Next Target : 00000053

---

My machine has a legitimate SRAT.  The kernel detects it has 4 nodes with
256 CPUs + 256G memory.

---

I don't know how to check whether the BIOS "should" or "should not" generate
CEDT.  I can check my machine if you let me know what to check specifically?

---

Yeah, and it is due to "there's a cost" if we don't compile out by default.

Hope that justifies?

reviews.author: Huang, Kai
reviews.summary:
Reviewer agreed that the patch is simpler, but suggested keeping Gregory's comment to provide additional information if needed.

Reviewer Huang, Kai expressed concern that disabling CXL_ACPI might not be feasible for some users due to BIOS limitations and suggested the patch is still worth implementing despite this issue.
reviews.raw_body:
Yeah agreed this is simpler.  Thanks for the suggestion.

I would like to honor Gregory's suggestion about the comment since it
provides more info if you agree?

(I also added "impacts certain NUMA userspace ABIs")

/*
 * Some platforms report CEDT CFMWS for hotplug device support. These
 * windows are unusable without CXL drivers, so don't reserve fake nodes
 * if they're compiled out - it wastes memory on per-node structures and
 * impacts certain NUMA userspace ABIs.
 */

---

Sure I can (I need to figure out how, though).  But I think it's still
useful for developers because some of them will tend to only enable Kconfig
options that they need to save build time.

And sometimes it's not quite easy to turn off CXL in the BIOS since the
machine would be mostly remote.

So I think this patch is still worthy to do?

2026-03-05.patch_summary: This patch modifies the ACPI NUMA code to only parse CFMWS (Compact Firmware Memory Window Specification) at boot when CXL_ACPI is enabled. This change prevents the loss of NUMA structure information, which would otherwise be lost if CXL_ACPI is built into the kernel. The approach taken is to conditionally create these structures based on the presence of CXL_ACPI.
